title: [Feature]: Add a hardened Oracle MySQL test database container
problem: The future Oracle MySQL sink needs a repeatable local database target before sink implementation starts. Developers need a short-lived Oracle MySQL database container that is safe for local tests, does not depend on long-lived credentials, uses Oracle Linux 9 slim as the container base, and can verify basic Oracle MySQL readiness, authentication, schema creation, inserts, reads, and cleanup behavior. Without a maintained test database container, Oracle MySQL sink development could drift into ad hoc local setups, stale manual instructions, hardcoded passwords, unsafe image choices, or tests that are difficult to reproduce.
proposal: Create a hardened test-only Oracle MySQL database container based on Oracle Linux 9 slim. The container should install and run the most recent generally available Oracle MySQL LTS or GA database version available from Oracle-supported channels at implementation time. As of 2026-05-25, Oracle MySQL 9.7.0 LTS is the current reference release to evaluate, but the implementation should keep the selected Oracle MySQL version explicit, documented, and easy to update. This feature should add a dedicated Dockerfile, local test scripts, deterministic unit tests, and optional Docker smoke tests that start a fresh short-lived Oracle MySQL container for each test run. It must not implement the Oracle MySQL sink itself; it should provide the safe local database backend needed for that future work.
users: Maintainers preparing the future Oracle MySQL sink, developers who need a reproducible local Oracle MySQL target, release engineers validating container test assets, and security reviewers checking that Oracle MySQL test credentials, ports, and data lifecycle are handled safely.
delivery_semantics: This feature does not change JetStream delivery behavior and must not add sink writes or ACK handling. Its purpose is to provide a local Oracle MySQL database target for future sink tests. When the future Oracle MySQL sink uses this target, commit-then-acknowledge semantics must still be verified separately by the sink tests.
security: Every Oracle MySQL test container run must generate fresh random credentials with Python `secrets` or equivalent cryptographically strong randomness. Test credentials must be short-lived, local-only, redacted from logs, excluded from issue comments, excluded from git, and removed when the container exits. The test scripts must avoid command-line password exposure where practical, must use random container names and host ports to avoid collisions, and must remove containers and anonymous volumes by default. A preserve flag may keep artifacts for debugging, but the default must clean up. The container must run with least privilege where Oracle MySQL allows it, avoid privileged mode, avoid Docker socket mounts, avoid host networking by default, expose only the required local Oracle MySQL port, and document all exceptions. The feature must never include production credentials, private endpoints, or real payload data.
acceptance: A dedicated Oracle MySQL test-container Dockerfile exists and uses Oracle Linux 9 slim as the only base image.
The selected Oracle MySQL database version is explicit, documented, and aligned with the latest Oracle MySQL LTS or GA release available from Oracle-supported channels at implementation time.
A local build script or test helper builds the Oracle MySQL test image without requiring private credentials or production configuration.
A local smoke test script starts a fresh short-lived Oracle MySQL container for every run, using a random container name, random host port, and freshly generated random credentials.

The smoke test waits for Oracle MySQL readiness, connects to the database, creates a dedicated test schema or table, inserts a row, reads it back, verifies the expected value, and exits with clear sanitized output.
The smoke test removes the Oracle MySQL container and temporary storage by default, with an explicit preserve flag for debugging.
No Oracle MySQL credentials, generated passwords, private endpoints, container IDs, or host-specific values are committed or printed in public test evidence.

Deterministic unit tests inspect the Dockerfile and scripts for Oracle Linux 9 slim base-image use, random credential generation, cleanup defaults, no shell injection, bounded arguments, and redaction behavior without requiring Docker.
Optional Docker-based integration tests are gated so normal unit tests do not require a local Docker daemon.

Documentation explains how the Oracle MySQL test container supports future Oracle MySQL sink development, how to run it locally, how credentials are generated, how cleanup works, what is intentionally test-only, and what remains out of scope.
The future Oracle MySQL sink issue is linked as related work, but the Oracle MySQL sink itself is not implemented under this issue.
tests: Add deterministic unit tests for the new Oracle MySQL test-container Dockerfile and scripts. Cover Oracle Linux 9 slim base-image enforcement, absence of non-Oracle base images, explicit Oracle MySQL version declaration, cryptographically strong random password generation, redacted output, random host-port allocation, random container-name construction, cleanup-by-default behavior, preserve-artifact flag behavior, fixed Docker command argument lists with `shell=False`, bounded timeouts, and clear operator errors. Add an optional local Docker smoke test that builds the image, starts a fresh Oracle MySQL container, waits for readiness, verifies authentication, creates a test schema or table, inserts and reads a record, and removes the container and storage by default. The smoke test must be safe to skip when Docker is unavailable and must not require live external Oracle MySQL services.
documentation: Add dedicated documentation for the Oracle MySQL test database container under the testing or deployment documentation tree. Include architecture and lifecycle diagrams, build commands, smoke-test commands, expected sanitized output, cleanup behavior, preserve-debug behavior, random credential handling, local port collision handling, troubleshooting, security boundaries, and clear wording that this is a test-only Oracle MySQL database backend for future Oracle MySQL sink development. Update README.md, docs/index.md, docs/testing.md, docs/docker.md, docs/sink-framework.md, docs/roadmap.md, ROADMAP.md, and CHANGELOG.md when implementation begins.
closeout: Close only after the release containing the Oracle MySQL test database container is published. Include release tag, selected Oracle MySQL version, Dockerfile path, script paths, tests run, Docker smoke evidence, documentation links, known limitations, and confirmation that the Oracle MySQL sink itself remains separate unless implemented under its own issue.
